message:
chore(beads): sync issue tracker state (saga/lease closures)

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.5 <noreply@anthropic.com>

@@ -368,12 +368,12 @@
 {"id":"asupersync-tmh.1","title":"Phase 4: Remote Tasks (Named Computations)","description":"# Phase 4: Remote Tasks (Named Computations)

## Purpose
Extend structured concurrency across process/network boundaries while remaining honest:
- no shipping closures to other machines
- remote execution is invoked by *named computations* with explicit capabilities

Remote tasks must still be owned by a local region:
- cancellation propagates
- region close waits for remote tasks (or escalates per lease/policy)

## Acceptance Criteria
- Remote tasks are represented as named computations (no closure shipping) with explicit handles.
- Remote handles participate in region quiescence (owned work; close implies no live remote children).
- Traces include enough metadata to explain remote lifecycle deterministically (within causal ordering constraints).

 {"id":"asupersync-tmh.1.1","title":"Define RemoteCap and spawn_remote API (named computations)","description":"# RemoteCap + spawn_remote API (Named Computations)

## Purpose
Expose remote execution via explicit capability:
- no closure shipping
- user requests a named computation to run remotely

## Design Sketch
- `RemoteCap` is a capability token inside `Cx`.
- `spawn_remote` takes:
  - node identifier
  - computation name (string/enum)
  - serialized inputs
- returns a handle owned by the region.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Remote operations are impossible without `RemoteCap`.
- Remote handles participate in region close/quiescence via leases.

 {"id":"asupersync-tmh.1.2","title":"Define remote protocol: spawn/ack/cancel/result/heartbeat","description":"# Remote Protocol: spawn/ack/cancel/result/heartbeat

## Purpose
Define the network protocol that implements remote structured concurrency.

## Required Messages
- Spawn request (includes computation name, inputs, lease info, idempotency key)
- Spawn ack (accepted/rejected)
- Result delivery (terminal outcome)
- Cancellation propagation
- Lease renewal / heartbeat

## Acceptance Criteria
- Protocol is fully specified, including error cases.
- Trace events represent remote message flow.

-{"id":"asupersync-tmh.2","title":"Phase 4: Leases + Idempotency + Sagas","description":"# Phase 4: Leases + Idempotency + Sagas

## Purpose
Provide the core distributed correctness tools:
- leases to bound orphan work
- idempotency keys for at-least-once messaging
- saga-style compensation as structured finalizers

These are the distributed equivalents of Phase 0 obligations and finalizers.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Lease + idempotency protocols prevent unbounded orphan remote work and enable safe retries.
- Sagas are represented as structured finalizers/compensations tied to region close.
- Protocol violations (e.g., commit vs abort conflicts) are surfaced deterministically in traces.

 {"id":"asupersync-tmh.2.1","title":"Implement Lease obligation (renew/expire/release)","description":"# Lease Obligation

## Purpose
Leases bound remote/orphan work:
- the owner must renew
- if lease expires, remote side cleans up

Leases are obligations: they must be released/expired (resolved) before region close.

## Semantics
- `Lease` corresponds to `ObligationKind::Lease`.
- Renewal extends expiry.
- Expiry triggers remote cleanup (fencing).

## Acceptance Criteria
- Leases are tracked in the obligation registry.
- Region close waits for lease resolution.

 {"id":"asupersync-tmh.2.2","title":"Implement IdempotencyKey and request dedup semantics","description":"# IdempotencyKey + Dedup Semantics

## Purpose
Distributed systems are at-least-once by default. Idempotency keys allow retries without duplicated effects.

## Requirements
- Key generation API.
- Protocol requires keys on remote spawn and effectful operations.
- Remote side deduplicates requests by key.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Retried spawn requests do not create duplicate remote work.
- Trace records dedup decisions.

-{"id":"asupersync-tmh.2.3","title":"Implement Saga framework (structured compensations)","description":"# Saga Framework (Structured Compensations)

## Purpose
Provide a structured way to express distributed cleanup:
- each step registers a compensation
- on abort/failure, compensations run in reverse order

In Asupersync terms, compensations are structured finalizers with explicit budgets.

## Requirements
- Step API that records both forward action and compensation.
- Deterministic execution of compensations.
- Trace records saga steps/compensations.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Compensations run exactly once, reverse order.
- Cancellation triggers saga abort path deterministically.

","status":"in_progress","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","owner":"jeff141421@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-01-16T07:20:35.054007427Z","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","updated_at":"2026-01-29T03:09:42.960762214Z","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"asupersync-tmh.2.3","depends_on_id":"asupersync-0cd","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-27T06:20:44Z","created_by":"import"},{"issue_id":"asupersync-tmh.2.3","depends_on_id":"asupersync-tmh.2","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-27T06:20:44Z","created_by":"import"}]}
-{"id":"asupersync-tmh.3","title":"Phase 4: Distributed Trace (Causal Ordering + Convergent State)","description":"# Phase 4: Distributed Trace (Causal Ordering + Convergent State)

## Purpose
Make distributed traces honest:
- represent time as a partial order (causal ordering), not a fake total order
- ensure obligation/lease state converges across replicas via join-semilattice rules

This is required for replay/debugging and for making distributed structured concurrency coherent.

## Core Elements
- Vector clocks (or equivalent causal metadata)
- Trace events that include node identity and causal metadata
- Obligation/lease state lattice with explicit conflict states

## Acceptance Criteria
- Trace metadata supports causal ordering (vector clocks or equivalent) so concurrent remote events remain unordered.
- Obligation/lease state converges via a join-semilattice (CRDT-style) with explicit conflict detection.
- Lab simulation can replay distributed traces deterministically up to the causal model.
","status":"open","priority":3,"issue_type":"feature","owner":"jeff141421@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-01-16T07:19:52.565194678Z","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","updated_at":"2026-01-16T08:06:07.189213685Z","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"asupersync-tmh.3","depends_on_id":"asupersync-tmh","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-27T06:20:44Z","created_by":"import"}]}
-{"id":"asupersync-tmh.3.1","title":"Implement vector clock (causal trace metadata)","description":"# Vector Clock (Causal Trace Metadata)

## Purpose
Distributed traces must preserve causal partial order:
- concurrent events remain unordered
- causality is explicit

Vector clocks are one standard representation.

## Requirements
- `VC: NodeId -> u64` representation.
- Operations:
  - increment local component
  - merge (componentwise max)
  - comparison: happens-before vs concurrent

## Acceptance Criteria
- Trace events carry vector clock.
- Tests validate ordering properties.

 {"id":"asupersync-tmh.3.2","title":"Define convergent obligation/lease state lattice (CRDT-style)","description":"# Convergent Obligation/Lease State Lattice

## Purpose
Distributed obligation state must converge across replicas without imposing a total order.

The spec calls out a join-semilattice view:
- `Reserved < Committed`
- `Reserved < Aborted`
- `Committed ⊔ Aborted = Conflict` (protocol violation)

## Requirements
- Define a join operation for obligation state.
- Explicitly represent conflict states.
- Trace and tooling surface conflicts deterministically.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Merging replicated obligation state is associative/commutative/idempotent.
- Conflicts are detectable.

 {"id":"asupersync-tmh.3.3","title":"Experiment: sheaf-theoretic consistency checks for distributed sagas","description":"# Experiment: Sheaf-Theoretic Consistency Checks

## Purpose
The design includes an advanced “global inconsistency detector” viewpoint:
- local states form a presheaf over network topology
- a globally consistent commit is a global section
- nontrivial cohomology (`H^1 != 0`) indicates an obstruction (split-brain-like inconsistency)

This task captures the experiment plan so it isn’t lost.

## Deliverables
- A simplified model for saga/lease state patches and overlaps.
- A diagnostic that reports “inconsistency” in a deterministic, debuggable way.

## Success Metrics
- Detects a constructed split-brain scenario that pairwise checks miss.

## Acceptance Criteria
- States a concrete distributed-saga consistency problem the sheaf lens is meant to detect (with a minimal example).
- Identifies the observable data to record (trace/overlap constraints) to support the check.
- Produces a minimal deterministic lab simulation test case (even if the check is stubbed initially).

 {"id":"asupersync-tmh.4","title":"Phase 4: Distributed Verification Suite (simulated network + fault injection)","description":"# Phase 4: Distributed Verification Suite (simulated network + fault injection)

## Purpose
Prove distributed semantics under controlled failure:
- partitions
- message loss/reordering
- remote crashes
- lease expiry

All tests should be deterministic and replayable using lab I/O simulation.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Known failure scenarios are reproducible via seed/schedule.
- Sagas run compensations deterministically.
